title: For the alumni of Mapa High

audience_match: Mapa High School alumni (Feb 23 speech audience + Inquirer readers)
Former Cong. Benny Abante Jr. (Alumni Assoc president)
Inquirer readership of Sunday Opinion column
Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity (FLP) donors + scholars
Manila Cathedral Foundation + Metrobank Foundation supporters
Poor Mapan students (8 subsistence scholarships pledged)
Filipino Catholic faithful (Pope John Paul II Pontifical Council disclosure)
Salonga's UP-Harvard-Yale community + UST + FEU alumni
Constitutional + legal scholars on CJ-appointment history
Philippine Stock Exchange + corporate boardroom community

primary_topics: Mapa High Alumni Association speech-summary biographical-disclosure column March 2, 2014
Speech context: former Cong Benny Abante Jr. (Mapa HS Alumni Assoc president) invited CJP; Feb 23 speech at Metro Tent Pasig City before 1,000 alumni; full speech at cjpanganiban.com self-citation
Poor-boy-to-CJ origin story: hawked newspapers, peddled cigarettes, shined shoes; promised himself to one day be president of Manila Times (largest-circulated daily then)

UP scholarship granted but family couldn't afford 15-centavo bus fare to QC campus; UST scholarship refused after CJP failed third Dominican-priest question 'Name them' â†’ 'Susmaryosep!' (Jesus, Mary and Joseph) self-deprecating 'religious ignoramus' confession
FEU scholarship without question; wanted chemical engineering but strict father ordered law; encountered Dean Jovito R. Salonga (UP + Harvard + Yale alum) at FEU

Cum laude graduate, 6th-placer bar exam, Yale scholarship granted but no transport money; practiced + taught law + dabbled in business; organized Baron Travel Corp.; five children all finished graduate degrees at Harvard, Stanford, UC, U. of Chicago, U. of Michigan

Catholic-faith intensification â†’ Second Plenary Council of Philippines invitation â†’ Pope John Paul II named CJP only Filipino member for 1996-2001 term of Pontifical Council for the Laity in the Vatican; 1991 part-time president of Inquirer

Supreme Court journey: June 17, 1992 FVR-presidential-elect offered DOJ Secretary; CJP declined 'thank you Mr. President but I am not worthy... I did not campaign for you' (in fact voted for Salonga); after two weeks FVR relented 'Ah sa Supreme Court ka na lang'; decade later GMA named CJP 21st Chief Justice of Philippines

Legal philosophy 'Liberty and Prosperity Under the Rule of Law' â€” justice and jobs / freedom and food / ethics and economics / democracy and development / liberty and prosperity must always go together (FLP signature six-paired-virtues)

Time-talent-treasure giving-back close: foundations chaired (Manila Cathedral Foundation + Metrobank Foundation + Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity); 75th-birthday two-years-ago â€” half retirement pay to FLP + one-year column income to Inquirer Foundation for Newsboys; today pledged 8 subsistence scholarships for poor Mapans; magnificent-tapestry-of-pains-and-gains closing with 'one constancy: presence, care and providence of our Lord Jesus Christ'
sub_topics: 'Please talk of how you became a sterling example of what a true-blue Mapan should be and how we can give back to society'
Was request of former congressman Benny Abante Jr., president of Mapa High School Alumni Association
CJP's full speech before 1,000 alumni gathered at Metro Tent, Pasig City, on Feb 23 may be accessed at cjpanganiban.com
Here's short version to fit in this space
Mostly poor were Mapa students of CJP's time
Looked at education and hard work as keys to success
CJP had to hawk newspapers, peddle cigarettes and shine shoes to support his study
Tired and weary, CJP promised himself he would one day be president of Manila Times, largest-circulated daily then
After high school University of Philippines granted CJP scholarship
But impoverished parents could not afford then 15-centavo bus fare to UP campus in Quezon City
Nearby University of Santo Tomas refused CJP scholarship
Because CJP failed to answer last of three questions of old Dominican priest:
First, 'How many Gods are there?'
Second, 'How many persons are there in one God?'
And third, tough question, 'Name them'
To which CJP sheepishly answered, 'Susmaryosep (Jesus, Mary and Joseph)!'
Yes, CJP was religious ignoramus
So CJP enrolled at Far Eastern University, which gave scholarship without any question
CJP wanted to be chemical engineer
But strict father ordered to take up law
Inconsolable was CJP's grief at inability to enroll at UP and to study chemical engineering
But life turned at FEU
There CJP met brilliant law dean Jovito R. Salonga
Who taught CJP insights from three schools he studied in: UP, Harvard and Yale
After graduating cum laude and placing sixth in bar exam
CJP was granted scholarship at Yale
But, like UP problem, did not have transport money
So, forthwith practiced and taught law, and dabbled in business
Organized Baron Travel Corp., which enabled CJP's five children to travel
And finish their graduate degrees at Harvard, Stanford, University of California, University of Chicago and University of Michigan
Also intensified study of Catholic faith
As result, invited to Second Plenary Council of Philippines
Later, named by Pope John Paul II as only Filipino member for 1996-2001 term of Pontifical Council for the Laity in the Vatican
In 1991, became part-time president of Inquirer
Like entry into law profession, CJP did not plan journey to Supreme Court
On June 17, 1992, then President-elect Fidel V. Ramos offered to be his secretary of justice
CJP respectfully declined: 'Thank you, Mr. President, but I am not worthy to join your Cabinet. I did not campaign for you during the last elections'
However, FVR was insistent: 'I know you did not campaign for me. In fact, you did not even vote for me. You voted for Jovy Salonga. But that is all right. I want a nonpartisan Cabinet'
After two weeks, FVR finally relented: 'Ah, sa Supreme Court ka na lang'
Decade later, President Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo named CJP 21st Chief Justice of Philippines
As jurist, espoused legal philosophy of 'Liberty and Prosperity Under the Rule of Law'
CJP believes justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, and liberty and prosperity must always go together
One is useless without other
After retirement in December 2006, embarked on new journey as independent director or adviser of some of largest firms listed in Philippine Stock Exchange
Also writes column in Inquirer
CJP asks successful co-alumni to give back to society by sharing time, talent and treasure
Sharing of time and talent is just as important as sharing of treasure
No one is so poor he cannot share something
And no one is so rich he does not need anything
CJP gives back time and talent by being chair or president of several foundations
Like Manila Cathedral Foundation, Metrobank Foundation and Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity
Sharing of treasure should really remain anonymous
So just mention two items already of public knowledge
To celebrate 75th birthday two years ago, CJP gave half of retirement pay to Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity
And one-year income as columnist to Inquirer Foundation for Newsboys
And today, may contribute eight subsistence scholarships for poor Mapans
To conclude, education, hard work and God transformed CJP
From poor newsboy to being president of most widely-read newspaper in country
From ignorant Catholic to being member of highest lay council of Church in Vatican
From frustrated applicant for graduate studies to being father of five wonderful children who each achieved CJP's impossible dream
From fumbling shoeshine boy to boardrooms of best corporate giants
From aspiring chemical engineer to reluctant lawyer
And, finally, to highest judicial position in country
As CJP contemplates life and moves toward its sunset
Knows that God has woven CJP's many pains and gains into magnificent tapestry showing His mystical presence
Truly, there is one constancy in CJP's life: presence, care and providence of our Lord Jesus Christ
To Him CJP dedicates all he was, all he is, and all he will ever be

signature_phrases.phrase: Liberty and Prosperity Under the Rule of Law
justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, and liberty and prosperity
presence, care and providence of our Lord Jesus Christ
sa Supreme Court ka na lang

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban writes one of his rare biographical-disclosure columns, summarizing his Mapa High School Alumni Association speech delivered the previous Sunday â€” Feb 23, 2014, at Metro Tent Pasig before 1,000 alumni. 'Please talk of how you became a sterling example of what a true-blue Mapan should be and how we can give back to society,' was the request of former Cong. Benny Abante Jr., president of the Mapa High School Alumni Association â€” CJ's full speech may be accessed at cjpanganiban.com; here's a short version to fit in this space. Mostly poor were the Mapa students of CJ's time; we looked at education and hard work as our keys to success â€” CJ had to hawk newspapers, peddle cigarettes and shine shoes to support his study; tired and weary, CJ promised himself he would one day be president of the Manila Times, the largest-circulated daily then. After high school the UP granted CJ a scholarship, but his impoverished parents could not afford the then 15-centavo bus fare to the UP campus in Quezon City. The nearby UST refused CJ a scholarship because he failed to answer the last of three questions of an old Dominican priest: first, 'How many Gods are there?'; second, 'How many persons are there in one God?'; and third, the tough question, 'Name them,' to which CJ sheepishly answered, 'Susmaryosep (Jesus, Mary and Joseph)!' â€” yes, CJ was a religious ignoramus. So CJ enrolled at FEU, which gave him a scholarship without any question; CJ wanted to be a chemical engineer but his strict father ordered him to take up law. Inconsolable was CJ's grief at his inability to enroll at UP and to study chemical engineering, but his life turned at FEU â€” there CJ met the brilliant law dean Jovito R. Salonga, who taught CJ his insights from the three schools he studied in: UP, Harvard and Yale. After graduating cum laude and placing sixth in the bar exam, CJ was granted a scholarship at Yale; but, like his UP problem, he did not have transport money â€” so, he forthwith practiced and taught law, and dabbled in business; CJ organized Baron Travel Corp., which enabled his five children to travel, and finish their graduate degrees at Harvard, Stanford, U. of California, U. of Chicago, and U. of Michigan. CJ also intensified his study of his Catholic faith â€” as a result, he was invited to the Second Plenary Council of the Philippines; later, he was named by Pope John Paul II as the only Filipino member for the 1996-2001 term of the Pontifical Council for the Laity in the Vatican; in 1991, he became part-time president of the Inquirer. Supreme Court journey: like his entry into the law profession, CJ did not plan his journey to the SC. On June 17, 1992, then President-elect Fidel V. Ramos offered CJ to be his Secretary of Justice; CJ respectfully declined, saying, 'Thank you, Mr. President, but I am not worthy to join your Cabinet; I did not campaign for you during the last elections.' However, FVR was insistent: 'I know you did not campaign for me; in fact, you did not even vote for me; you voted for Jovy Salonga; but that is all right; I want a nonpartisan Cabinet.' After two weeks, FVR finally relented: 'Ah, sa Supreme Court ka na lang.' A decade later, President GMA named CJ the 21st Chief Justice of the Philippines. As a jurist, CJ espoused his legal philosophy of 'Liberty and Prosperity Under the Rule of Law' â€” CJ believes justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, and liberty and prosperity must always go together; one is useless without the other. After his retirement in December 2006, he embarked on a new journey as an independent director or adviser of some of the largest firms listed in the PSE, and also writes a column in the Inquirer. Giving back: CJ asks his successful co-alumni to give back to society by sharing their time, talent and treasure â€” the sharing of time and talent is just as important as the sharing of treasure; no one is so poor he cannot share something, and no one is so rich he does not need anything. CJ gives back his time and talent by being chair or president of several foundations, like the Manila Cathedral Foundation, Metrobank Foundation, and FLP. The sharing of treasure should really remain anonymous, so CJ will just mention two items already of public knowledge: to celebrate his 75th birthday two years ago, CJ gave half of his retirement pay to FLP and his one-year income as a columnist to the Inquirer Foundation for Newsboys; and today, may CJ contribute eight subsistence scholarships for poor Mapans. To conclude, education, hard work and God transformed CJ from a poor newsboy to being president of the most widely-read newspaper in our country; from an ignorant Catholic to being a member of the highest lay council of the Church in the Vatican; from a frustrated applicant for graduate studies to being father of five wonderful children who each achieved CJ's impossible dream of graduating from a pedigreed US university; from a fumbling shoeshine boy to the boardrooms of the best corporate giants; from an aspiring chemical engineer to a reluctant lawyer; and, finally, to the highest judicial position in our country. As CJ contemplates his life and moves toward its sunset, he knows that God has woven his many pains and gains into a magnificent tapestry showing His mystical presence â€” truly, there is one constancy in his life: the presence, care and providence of our Lord Jesus Christ; to Him CJ dedicates all he was, all he is, and all he will ever be.
